content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 20,
1993
Mr. Joe Mark
322 34th Street
Hermosa Beach,
CA
Mr.
Bob Brigham
1271 Bryant
Manhattan Beach,
CA
Mr.
Jim Duffy
547 Marine
Manhattan Beach,
CA
RE:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—93-139
Dear Messrs.
Mark,
Brigham,
Duffy:
You have requested advice regarding the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).l/
Since your
request refers to conduct that has already occurred,
we cannot
provide you with formal advice concerning that conduct.
Regulation 18329(b) (9) (A)
provides that formal written advice will
be declined where the “requestor is seeking advice relating to
past conduct.”
However,
we hope the following informal assistance
will be helpful.2/
OUESTION
You have asked what the reporting requirements are under the
Act for individuals who received contributions and made
expenditures for the purpose of opposing a local ballot measure in
the November 3,
1992, election.
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322.5660


Messrs. Mark,
Brigham,
and Duffy
Advice No.
1-93-139
Page
2
CONCLUSION
An individual does not have a campaign filing obligation
until he/she qualifies as a “committee” under the Act.
Based on
the facts presented in your letter, it appears you did not qualify
as a committee and therefore were not required to file campaign
disclosure statements in connection with the November 3,
1992
election.
FACTS
You
(Mr. Joe Mark,
Mr.
Bob Brigham,
and Mr.
Jim Duffy)
opposed Proposition V in a November 3,
1992 local election.
Independently of one another,
you made expenditures out of your
personal funds of less than $1,000 to produce flyers and signs and
to place ads, etc., opposing this proposition.
You individually
directed your payments to the various vendors.
In addition,
acting independently of one another,
you received contributions
totaling less than $1,000.
ANALYSIS
Section 83013 defines the term “committee” as any person or
combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more
in a calendar year;
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling $1,000
or more in a calendar year;
or
(c)
Makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more in
a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or
committees.
To qualify as a “recipient” committee under Section 82013(a),
a person must receive contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year.
The group would have qualified as a recipient
committee if, collectively,
you pooled your funds and spent $1,000
or more for a single product or service, or any number of products
or services to oppose the proposition.
Or, if any individual


Messrs.
Mark,
Brigham,
and Duffy
Advice No.
1—93—139
Page
3
had received contributions from other persons which,
combined with
the payments made from that individual’s personal funds had
totaled $1,000 or more, that individual would have qualified as a
recipient committee.
(Madden Advice Letter,
No.
A-85--223,
and
Rynearson Advice Letter,
No. A-90-695, copies enclosed.)
To qualify as an “independent expenditure” committee pursuant
to Section 82013(b),
a person must make expenditures totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year in connection with
communications which expressly advocate the election or defeat of
a clearly identified candidate or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a clearly identified measure,
or taken as a whole and in
context, unambiguously urge a particular result in an election but
which are not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate
or committee.
(Section 82031.)
A payment is made at the behest of a candidate or committee
if it is made under the control or at the direction of,
in
cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with or at the
request or suggestion of the candidate or committee.
(Regulation
Sections 18215 and 18225.)
To qualify as a “major donor” committee pursuant to Section
82013 (c),
a person must make expenditures to or at the behest of
candidates or committees totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar
year.
In summary,
based on the information you have provided
concerning your financial activity during calendar year 1992,
you
did not qualify as a committee and you were not required to file
campaign disclosure statements.
If you have further questions concerning this letter, please
contact me at 916/322—5662.
Sincerely,
Mary A
Kvasager
U
Political Reform Consultant
